title: Rosewater Turkish delight
subtitle: Cornflour-set sugar squares with rosewater and pistachios
excerpt: Turkish delight (lokum) in the Ottoman household tradition: a syrup of sugar and lemon thickened with a cornflour-and-water slurry, flavoured with rosewater and pink colour, set in a tin, cut into squares and dusted with icing sugar and cornflour. Forty pieces.
sourceNotes: Turkish delight (lokum) developed at the court of the Ottoman Empire in the eighteenth century. The cornflour-and-sugar method is documented in Turkish household manuals from the nineteenth century onward. Florence White, Good Things in England (1932): notes the English household adoption from the late Victorian period.

recipe.freezeNotes: Turkish delight goes sticky on defrost; keep in a tin at cool room temperature, layered with baking paper.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Set the slab overnight in the tin; cut and dust the next day. Keeps 3 weeks.

baking.bakeTemperatureNote: soft-ball stage, 115°C target sugar temperature for the syrup

recipeTools.notes: 20 cm square, oiled and lined with baking paper.

glossaryTerms.definition: Turkish delight thickens by long, slow cooking of a hot sugar syrup added gradually to a cornflour-water slurry. The mixture is stirred constantly over a low heat for 45–60 minutes; the starch swells and the mixture turns from cloudy white to translucent gold. Stop stirring and the bottom catches; the catch flavours the whole batch with a burnt note. Patience is the only path to a clean set.

content.text: Turkish delight is a study in patience: a hot sugar syrup cooked to soft-ball is poured slowly into a cornflour-and-water slurry, then stirred over a low heat for nearly an hour as the starch swells and the mixture turns translucent. The cooked paste is poured into a tin to set overnight, then cut and dusted in icing sugar and cornflour.

Two notes on technique: the syrup must be hot and the slurry must be hot when they meet — pour the syrup in a thin stream while whisking. After the merge, switch to a wooden spoon and stir the bottom of the pan constantly; if the mixture catches at any point, the whole batch tastes burnt.

attrs.title: Hot syrup is dangerous
attrs.body: The syrup reaches 115°C. If hot syrup touches skin, run cold water over it for at least ten minutes and seek medical care if needed.

content.text: Syrup to soft-ball, slurry to thick

Oil and line a 20 cm square tin. Combine the {{caster-sugar}}, first 400 ml of {{water}} and juice of the {{lemon}} in a saucepan; bring to a boil, then cook to 115°C on a thermometer. Meanwhile, whisk the first {{cornflour}}, {{cream-of-tartar}} and the second 500 ml of {{water}} in a heavy pan until smooth. Bring to a low simmer, stirring constantly; the slurry should thicken to a paste in about 5 minutes.

When the syrup reaches 115°C, pour it in a slow steady stream into the slurry, whisking. Reduce the heat to very low. Switch to a wooden spoon and stir constantly across the bottom and sides of the pan for 45–55 minutes; the mixture will turn from cloudy white to translucent pale gold, and a spoon dragged across the base will leave a trail that slowly fills back in. Stir in the {{rose-water}} and the chopped {{pistachios}} if using.

Pour into the prepared tin and level the surface with an oiled palette knife. Leave to set at cool room temperature for at least 12 hours, or overnight. Sift the {{icing-sugar}} and second {{cornflour}} together in a wide shallow bowl. Cut the slab into 40 squares with an oiled knife; toss each piece in the dust to coat. Store in an airtight tin layered with baking paper.
